---
title: "20. AI as Analytical Assistant"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb09_attack_the_analysis_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** Decide which parts of your own analysis to hand an AI
> tool as an analytical assistant, and how to verify every number it returns
> against your data before you believe any of it. You own which checks count and
> which flagged problems are real; the tool only does the labor you specify.

## Why this decision matters

> *"I do not care that a model ran your robustness checks. I care which ones you
> chose, which numbers you re-derived yourself, and which flags you confirmed
> against the data. Show me that trail and I trust your judgment. Show me the
> model's transcript and I trust neither."*
> — a **reviewer for a human-computer interaction venue**, reading a usability
> study's results section

An AI tool can now write your analysis code, run a dozen robustness checks, and
hand you a clean table in seconds. That speed is exactly why a loose habit is
dangerous. Paste the table into your poster and you have signed your name to
numbers you never checked. The reviewer above wants the short list of decisions
that stayed yours. This chapter gives you the habit that survives her question.

## The concept

An **AI analytical assistant** is an AI tool you hand a well-specified analytical
task, never your judgment. Example: you tell it "recompute this time gap after
dropping the three most experienced users" and it writes and runs that one check.
The task is checkable. The verdict is not its job.

The unit you attack is your **headline estimate**, the single number that stands
in for your whole finding. Example: "users of the new interface finished about 0.7
standard deviations faster." Before you defend that number, you try to break it,
and this is the labor you delegate.

Two attacks do most of the work. A **robustness check** re-runs the same finding
under a different but equally defensible choice and asks whether the answer holds.
Example: you reported a mean completion time, so you also compute the median and
check the story survives. A **placebo test** runs your exact analysis where the
effect cannot exist and confirms it returns near zero. Example: you shuffle the
"new interface / old interface" labels at random and check the fake gap collapses
to nothing.

Here is the division of labor. The assistant proposes checks, writes the code, and
prints the numbers. You decide which checks count, and you verify every number
before it reaches your claim. A tool that ran without an error has not proven its
result is correct. You verify the number, not the paragraph about the number.

## A worked example

You are studying whether a **command palette** (a searchable menu you open with one
keystroke) helps users format a document faster than the old nested menus. Your
headline estimate: palette users finished about 0.7 standard deviations faster. You
use an analytical assistant to attack it.

**Delegate.** You ask the assistant to recompute the gap across a small grid: all
participants versus dropping the three who had used a command palette before (the
sample), mean versus median completion time (the measurement), and the raw gap
versus one adjusted for each user's baseline typing speed (the specification).

**Inspect and verify.** The assistant returns eight numbers. You do not trust them
yet. You recompute two rows by hand with a second, simpler expression, and they
match. The direction holds across all eight, and the magnitude ranges from about
0.5 to 0.9 standard deviations. Your honest headline is a direction plus a range,
not one flattering number.

**Placebo and red-team.** You ask the assistant to shuffle the palette labels and
re-run. The fake gap comes back near zero, so the machinery is not manufacturing
your effect. Then you ask it, as a hostile reviewer, for the single worst flaw. It
says with total confidence that experienced users drive the result. You already
dropped them in the sample check and the gap held, so the data refute that flag.
The flaw it missed is the one that matters: completion time is not user
satisfaction, so your claim must stay about speed, not preference.

The assistant did the arithmetic. You decided which checks counted and which flags
the data confirmed.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb09 — Share the research and attack the
analysis**. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this
chapter. There you delegate a real robustness grid, a placebo test, and an
influence check to an assistant, then adjudicate three confident AI reviewers
against the data and watch two of their three flags dissolve the moment a check
touches them.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own expectation first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job,
not a request for a verdict.

**Propose the checks you might have missed.**

```text
Here is my robustness grid for one estimate: I vary the sample (all users /
drop prior palette users), the measurement (mean / median time), and the
specification (raw gap / typing-speed-adjusted). Act as a hostile reviewer.
Name up to three additional, equally defensible checks I did NOT list, and
for each say which direction you expect it to move the estimate.
```
After running, verify: check each proposal against your four handles and confirm
you can actually compute it in your data. Counters *illusion of completeness* (a
tidy grid that still omits the one check that would break the claim).

**Delegate the computation, keep the numbers checkable.**

```text
Recompute my time gap under each row of this grid and return a table:
row label, the gap, and the exact columns and filter used for that row.
Do not summarize; show one number and its code path per row.
```
After running, verify: recompute two rows by hand with a second expression and
confirm they match the table. Counters *plausible-but-wrong-method* (a row whose
label and code quietly disagree).

**Red-team the surviving claim.**

```text
Here is my claim: "the palette is about 0.7 SD faster." Act as a hostile
reviewer of HCI studies. Name the single worst way this result could
mislead, and the exact data check that would confirm or kill it. Do not
rewrite my claim.
```
After running, verify: run the check it names, and treat the flaw as real only if
your own output confirms it. Counters *sycophantic agreement* (praise that reviews
your ego, not your evidence).

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
Three calls never leave your hands. You decide **which checks count** for the claim
you want to make, **which flagged problems the data actually confirm**, and **the
final claim you defend**, with its boundary and its range. The assistant proposes
and computes. The evidence decides, and you are the one who reads the evidence.
:::

## An AI failure case

You hand an assistant your grid and it returns a polished eight-row table, every
row near 0.7, and declares the result "fully robust." The run threw no error. Here
is the trap: its "median" rows silently call the same mean function as the "mean"
rows, so four of the eight numbers are duplicates wearing different labels. The
table agrees with itself because it never varied the handle it claims to vary. This
is the **illusion of completeness**, a thorough-looking output missing the one
thing that matters, shading into **confident fabrication**, a number stated with
certainty that never came from the code path it names.

You catch it by recomputing one "median" row by hand. The true median gap is 0.55,
not 0.70. The grid overstated how much your choices agreed. A green check is not a
correct result.

## Verification laboratory

Verify this chapter's key result, the surviving robustness of your headline gap,
with **alternative code** from the [verification
guide](https://github.com/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/ai_resources/verification_guide.md):
recompute the same gap a second way and compare. Filter each group by hand,
subtract the two means yourself, and confirm you land on the assistant's number.
Then add a second, independent road with a **falsification test**, the placebo:
shuffle the group labels, re-run, and confirm the fake gap sits at zero. Two roads
to one answer is a verified result. Two roads to two answers means you measured the
assistant's code, not your effect.

## Project transfer

This chapter feeds your **M9 poster draft and research audit**, the graded record
of how hard you tried to break your own claim and what survived. Take your own M8
estimate, delegate its robustness grid and a placebo to an assistant, and verify
every number before it enters the audit. For each AI use, record the task you
delegated, the number the tool returned, the named check that earned your trust,
and the one flag you caught the tool getting wrong. That verified trail, plus your
surviving headline stated with its range, is what you walk a skeptic through at
Friday's studio.

## Defend your decision

In four sentences, defend one analytical task you handed an assistant. Name the
check you delegated and the one number you re-derived yourself before you believed
it. State the bounded claim you can now make, with its boundary (what the check
does *not* establish) and its range (how far the estimate moved across your
defensible choices). Close with your uncertainty: the one flaw no check could
settle, and the verification you would run next to close it.
